posts.name: Paul Gordon
posts.cooked:
Maybe it is my English upbringing, but I really geek out on the weather. This year we had access to a weather station at the vineyard and love comparing the conditions to the Northern Rhone.
Below is a comparison of the Ampius monthly average temps vs Halcon. I do not have a long-term history for the vineyard so I extrapolated from the temps in Boonville/Ukiah. I took the deviation from historical average for Boonville as a percentage and multiplied the 2015 monthly average from Halcon.

http://halconvineyards.com/Halcon%20Averages

Below is a comparison of the monthly averages for Halcon 2015 vs Ampuis 2015 vs Ampuis historical average. It will be interesting to see the character of the 2015 Northern Rhones as it was certainly very warm throughout the year (although it did moderate in September).
At Halcon you can see the cool May was followed by a warm June. All year we had 16 days above 90F, 6 of those were in June, more than any other month, and the highest temp for the year (97F) was 6/30.
Summer was close to average, with September a little warmer than average. October was significantly above average, but that was moot as we picked our last fruit on the 4th.

http://www.halconvineyards.com/2015%20temps

Interesting charts Paul.

It would be interesting to see a comparison of sunlight hours, moisture/rain, and exposure too, not to mention vineyard management. But what you have is pretty surprising, at least to me. Thanks for sharing.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: If you put in a bunch of power lines and build a nuclear power plant at the end of the valley, the places will be almost identical.

posts.name: Loren Sonkin
posts.cooked: Very cool. I need to circle back to this and spend some time. Thanks.

posts.name: Paul Gordon
posts.cooked:
GregT:

Interesting charts Paul.

It would be interesting to see a comparison of sunlight hours, moisture/rain, and exposure too, not to mention vineyard management. But what you have is pretty surprising, at least to me. Thanks for sharing.

Greg

The biggest difference would be rainfall - as you know CA is a desert in the summer. That dryness results in thicker skinned berries and reduced disease pressures.
We experience a lot of wind that further reduces those pressures - we sprayed sulfur just once the last two vintages.
I need to collect the sunlight hour numbers.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Craig G:

If you put in a bunch of power lines and build a nuclear power plant at the end of the valley, the places will be almost identical.

LOL. Much of N. Rhone is not particularly attractive. Halcon, OTOH, is.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Paul, have you got daytime max/nightime min temps?

posts.name: Paul Gordon
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

Paul, have you got daytime max/nightime min temps?

I do. Let me create the graphs. At Halcon we get a slightly wider swing than Ampuis (but less of a swing than Boonville).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: How about humidity?

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:
Craig G:

If you put in a bunch of power lines and build a nuclear power plant at the end of the valley, the places will be almost identical.

You left out the rail line running next to the cellars to provide necessary vibration.

posts.name: John Cabot
posts.cooked: I’ve noticed the same thing up here in Orleans on the Klamath River. Our temps are very similar to Ampuis, particularly regarding fall temps. The schist shale soils we have are strikingly similar as well on our north sloped Kimberly’s vineyard.

posts.name: Peter Hickner
posts.cooked:
I’m curious to know if harvest dates are later for Halcon vs. Ampuis, taking advantage of the warm fall.


P Hickner

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked: Also there’s a lot of cigarette smoke in the environment in Ampuis.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
Oliver McCrum:

Also there’s a lot of cigarette smoke in the environment in Ampuis.

I suppose in Anderson Valley it’s mostly bongs.

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked: There is that. Terroir of one kind or another.

posts.name: Paul Gordon
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

How about humidity?

We will be lower than Ampuis and a lot less rain mid-June thru mid-Sept.

posts.name: Paul Gordon
posts.cooked:
PeterH:

I’m curious to know if harvest dates are later for Halcon vs. Ampuis, taking advantage of the warm fall.


P Hickner

Yes Peter. In years 2008-13 the pick dates for our Syrah spanned October 11th to October 29th. The critical last 3-4 weeks are the same temps/slightly cooler than Ampuis.
Btw in 2014-15 we did pick late September.

posts.name: Paul Gordon
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

Paul, have you got daytime max/nightime min temps?

See average monthly high and low temps for Ampuis and Halcon here - http://www.halconvineyards.com/2015%20Halcon%20Ampuis%20Hi%20Lo%20Temps

posts.name: Joe Webb
posts.cooked:
Paul Gordon:

Below is a comparison of the Ampius monthly average temps vs Halcon. I do not have a long-term history for the vineyard so I extrapolated from the temps in Boonville/Ukiah. I took the deviation from historical average for Boonville as a percentage and multiplied the 2015 monthly average from Halcon.

Paul really cool to see this data. I have had a weather station on site for a couple years now and need to do more comparisons like your doing. Great work!

You mentioned using some temp data from Boonville, where did you get your info? The only public one I am aware of is the NOAA one above CDF at 644’ elevation were as I am at about 410’. There is a huge difference as the valley is so small, we see an average of 50+ degree swings daily during the growing season. If you load Boonville into weather.com/iphone you get weather for the Ukiah airport typically 10+ degrees hotter daily and -10 not cold enough for nights during the growing season. I have been trying with not a lot of luck to get a station at the airport so that it will be the official one with accurate data from more central Boonville.

Yes I use the NOAA numbers. Would love to see some other data. It would be very interesting to compare some of the vineyards of Anderson Valley to Burgundy.
